and sculpted scenes which he views on monuments—are represented

... a mile long. 
Pruned in such a way as to have windows here and there, the walls of the hedge afford the 
strolling visitor a view either into the surrounding country landscape or down onto the court- 
yard framed by the fragrant walls. In the courtyard’s center is a beautiful fountain made up of 
nymphs from whose breasts the water spurts (fig. 20). The fourth side of the perfectly equilat- 
eral enclosure is the facade of 
the palace of the queen. It comprises no less than two hundred 
columns, arranged in four rows of fifty apiece in a chromatic display of Numidian, Claudian, 
Simidian,...

... one of the most important perspec-
tives on dreams in the second century CE—and in Apuleius' novel. 
(g) The telepathic dimension indicates those dream images which refer 
to what is in the mind of another at a distance, information which we could 
not know through the ordinary channels of the senses. Like precognition, this 
dimension is difficult to comprehend, but the data indicates the reality of such 
phenomena,19 
(h) The spirit-world dimension is one of the oldest recognized. The 
Babylonians and Assyrians, for example, believed that bad dreams are caused 
by spirits of the dead...

<!-- Page 107 -->

The Religious Dreamworld of Apuleius' Metamorpho\< ν 
is a portrayal of Psyche as the vessel of individuation—one who gives spe-
cific form to the constantly moving energy of life without being shattered by 
its overwhelming power. Neumann maintains that Psyche's first three tasks 
contribute to...

... Mitchell has argued, could have been influenced by the Medusa’s head on 
the temple of the Hadrian at Cizicus, documented by Ciriaco d’Ancona. Similarly, the golden 
grapevines on the roof of the temple of Venus could also come from Ciriaco’s drawing of the 
temple of Cizicus, whose portico with five columns was decorated with grapevines.'!* 
The gigantomachia on the plinth of the pyramid can be seen as deriving from the frieze of 
the Parthenon describing the victory of the Greeks over the Persians, 
a monument that very 
few Italian humanists besides Ciriaco would have known first-hand....

<!-- Page 211 -->

In the context of this study, it is the anthropomorphic epiphor in storytelling that 
serves as such a password. This is the framework in which the Hypnerotomachia and the new 
erotic imperative it propounds with regard to architecture must be understood. As we will 
have many occasions to see in...

<!-- Page 240 -->

For all the strangeness of these buildings emerging out of those metaphors, the 
main feeling they evoke in the beholders is another component of marvel: unabashed delight. 
“Gaude mi, carissima mater” (rejoice for me, dearest mother), exclaims the fictive Alexander 
the Great about the ingenious wonders...

ANTHONY BLUNT 
have been of another weaving.1 One of them represented the scene shown 
in the Rouen painting. The second showed almost certainly the scene of 
the Czernin canvas.2 The third represented Poliphilus and Polia in an 
orchard, with nymphs picking flowers. This is the scene described at the 
very end of the first book of the Songe and shown in the engraving on p. I3 ib 
of B6roalde's translation. We are therefore able to identify four out of the
